sections_parsed.abstract:
Background: Dental accreditation bodies are increasingly being asked to define how artificial 
intelligence (AI) should be incorporated into undergraduate and postgraduate curricula. 
Although the technology dimension of accreditation has expanded over the past decade , the 
evidence needed to guide AI specific decisions remains fragmented across capability studies, 
attitude surveys, and small educational pilots, with no synthesis focused on accreditation policy. 
 Aim: To map the empirical evidence informing AI competency frameworks, curriculum 
integration, and educator readiness in dental education, and to determine what this evidence can 
and cannot support at the level of accreditation requirements. 
Methods. This scoping review followed JBI methodology and is reported in accordance with 
PRISMA-ScR. PubMed was searched for studies published between 1 January 2018 and 30 April 
2026 using a sensitive strategy combining AI and dental  education terms. After screening 488 
records against eligibility criteria focused on accreditation and curriculum reform, 28 studies 
were included. A standardised extraction template captured setting, design, sample, AI tool, 
educational outcome, quantitat ive findings, limitat ions, and Kirkpatrick level, operationalised 
according to Yardley and Dornan (2012). The synthesis was organised into six tiers reflecting the 
distinct evidential roles of included studies. 
Results. Four findings dominate the evidence base. First, a global competency framework for AI 
in dentistry was first published in 2022; however, subsequent reviews suggest that integration 
into curricula has been uneven and frequently limited in depth. Second, faculty readiness, rather 
than student interest or AI capability, appears to be the principal constraint on implementation: 
across surveys in Pakistan, the United States, the Netherlands, and multinational cohorts, less 
than 30% of teaching staff regularly employ AI in assessment, and emerging evidence links lower 
levels of perceived faculty readiness to poorer student performance metrics . Third, intervention 
evidence is mixed: the sole randomised controlled trial found that skills acquired during AI  
assisted training did not persist when support was removed, while the only controlled experiment 
involving explainable AI revealed evidence of automation bias; favourable intervention outcomes 
were constrained by industry authorship, recruitment at a single institution, possible novelty bias, 

Page 2 
or dependence on self-reported rather than objectively measured endpoints. Fourth, no included 
intervention reached Kirkpatrick Level 3 or 4, meaning the evidence base speaks to reactions and 
short-term learning but not to sustained behaviour change or patient outcomes. 
Conclusion. The literature supports requiring AI literacy at the knowledge level, including 
critical appraisal, automation-bias mitigation, and ethics, while treating applied competencies as 
recommended rather than mandatory until stronger evidence becomes available. Accreditation 
standards that compel AI integration without parallel investment in staff development risk 
imposing an unsupported obligation. Given the contextual variation across the included studies, 
standards are better framed as outcome -based expectations with locally adaptable 
implementation pathways than as fixed curricular content prescriptions. 
Keywords. Artificial intelligence; dental education; accreditation; curriculum; competency 
framework; scoping review; PRISMA-ScR. 
  

Page 3
sections_parsed.introduction_rationale:
The technology dimension of dental accreditation 
Accreditation in dental education is the external mechanism through which professional and 
educational standards are evaluated against agreed criteria, with consequences for institutional 
standing, program recognition, and graduate registration (Field, 1995; Schönwetter et al., 2026). 
Accrediting bodies have historically emphasised inputs such as faculty numbers and 
infrastructure, processes such as teaching quality and assessment validity, and outputs such as 
clinical competence and professionalism. Over the past decade, however, many frameworks have 
begun to incorporate a technology dimension, setting explicit expectations about digital 
environments, digital competencies, and the educational technologies institutions should provide 
and graduates should demonstrate (Dzyuba et al., 2025; Nafea, 2021). 
This development reflects the normalisation of digital tools in clinical dentistry. CAD/CAM, cone 
beam imaging, intraoral scanning, electronic health records, and teledentistry have shifted from 
optional adjuncts to expected components of practice (Dzyuba et al., 2025). Accreditation has 
generally followed this shift, though often with delay and limited specificity: standards 
increasingly mention digital competence, yet they frequently remain broad, requiring graduates 
to use contemporary technologies witho ut defining which tools, what level of mastery, or how 
achievement should be assessed (Field, 1995). 
Artificial intelligence presents a more difficult version of this challenge. In dentistry, AI 
encompasses diverse applications  like convolutional neural networks (CNN) for image 
interpretation, large language models (LLM) for clinical reasoning support and communication, 
and rule based decision tools for treatment planning. These systems differ in purpose, evidential 
maturity, and risk profile; standards written for digital technology in general are unlikely to be 
sufficient for AI in particular. The educat ional implications also differ: AI use requires critical 
appraisal of probabilistic outputs, recognition of automation bias, and ethical reasoning under 
uncertainty, rather than operational competence with a digital device. 
The accreditation dilemma 
This creates what we term the accreditation dilemma: standards must be specific enough to be 
auditable, yet the evidence base for what AI competencies dental graduates need , and what 
educational approaches reliably develop them , remains limited and uneven (Schwendicke et al., 
2022; Aura-Tormos et al., 2026). Overly prescriptive standards risk codifying tools or teaching 
approaches that the evidence does not yet justify, and which may quickly become obsolete. 
Standards that are too vague risk becoming symbol ic, allowing institutions to appear compliant 
without making meaningful curriculum change. 

Page 4 
This dilemma is not unique to AI, nor to dental education. Accrediting authorities operating in 
this space include the Commission on Dental Accreditation in the United States, the General 
Dental Council in the United Kingdom, the Australian Dental Council,  the Canadian Dental 
Accreditation Commission, the Association for Dental Education in Europe framework, and 
national bodies such as the National Centre for Academic Accreditation and Evaluation in Saudi 
Arabia, the Dental Council of India, and the Nationa l Health Commission of China (Field, 1995; 
Nafea, 2021). These bodies differ in regulatory authority and in the degree to which they prescribe 
technology-related standards, but they face the same central question: how should AI appear in 
accreditation when the evidence base is still developing? 
Existing literature and remaining gap 
Early literature has begun to address this question. Schwendicke et al. proposed a four -domain 
AI core curriculum covering terminology and machine learning reasoning, use cases and software 
setup, evaluation metrics and impact, and generalisability, accoun tability, and governance 
(Schwendicke et al., 2022). Surveys have explored student and educator attitudes, while small 
intervention studies have tested specific tools and teaching formats (Uribe et al., 2024; Kavadella 
et al., 2026; Glick et al., 2022; Schropp et al., 2024; Caldwell et al., 2026; Kavadella et al., 2024). 
Recent reviews have mapped parts of the field, but none has focused specifically on what the 
literature can justify at the level of accreditation (Aura -Tormos et al., 2026; Kong et al., 202 5; 
Iniesta and Pérez-Higueras, 2026). 
That distinction matters. Evidence that an AI system performs well on a dental task is not 
equivalent to evidence that students should be required to learn that system, or that schools 
should be accredited on that basis (Lafourcade et al., 2025; Ji et al., 2026). Capability findings are 
sometimes used as though they were curricular findings, even though the educational and 
regulatory implications differ. 
Review question and objectives 
This scoping review addresses the question: What empirical evidence currently informs the 
development of AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation 
standards in undergraduate and postgraduate dental education? 
Two subsidiary questions guide the review: first, what does the evidence reveal about educator 
and learner readiness for AI integration; second, which educational interventions using AI have 
been tested, and at which levels of educational outcome? Our objectives are to identify defensible 
AI competencies suitable for accreditation, to characterise educator readiness as a condition for 
feasible implementation, and to clarify the methodological boundaries of existing evidence so that 
policy recommendations remain proportional to available data. 
 

Page 5 
Methods 
Approach 
This scoping review followed JBI methodology and is reported according to PRISMA -ScR 
guidelines (Peters et al., 2020; Tricco et al., 2018). We adapted the Arksey and O'Malley 
framework, as refined by Levac et al., which allows iterative refinement of the r esearch question, 
inclusion criteria, and synthesis approach (Arksey and O'Malley, 2005; Levac et al., 2010). We 
chose a scoping rather than systematic review design to map and characterise a diverse body of 
evidence rather than synthesise effect sizes (Peters et al., 2020). 
The review was prospectively registered on the Open Science Framework before screening began 
(OSF; https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/U89WE).
sections_parsed.methods_eligibility:
Studies were included if they met all four criteria. First, participants or settings had to be dental 
students, dental educators, or dental schools. Second, the study had to address artificial 
intelligence in any form including machine learning (ML) , deep learning, large language models 
(LLM), generative AI, or AI assisted decision support tools. Third, the educational context had to 
be undergraduate dental education, postgraduate training, or continuing professional 
development; we excluded clinical AI ac curacy studies unless they explicitly addressed 
educational implications. Fourth, studies had to be directly relevant to accreditation or 
curriculum reform. We considered this criterion met if a study proposed, evaluated, or directly 
informed an AI compete ncy framework; stated curriculum integration, accreditation alignment, 
or educational policy as its explicit purpose; constituted an empirical study of educational needs 
with explicit curricular recommendations and a sample of at least 30 participants; des cribed an 
educational intervention intended for institutional implementation; or was a foundational 
position paper widely cited by accrediting bodies or curriculum committees. 
The following were excluded: large language model performance studies (e.g., LLM performance 
on multiple-choice examinations) that did not propose curricular implications; single class pilots 
with fewer than 30 participants and no discussion of institutional integration; AI versus clinician 
accuracy comparisons without educational framing; knowledge, attitude, and practice surveys 
without explicit curricular recommendations or with fewer than 100 participants; bibliometric or 
scoping reviews that did not di rectly inform curriculum design; conference abstracts, letters, 
editorials, and other non-peer reviewed material; and non-English language publications without 
an English full text version. 
Three commentary or position papers were referenced in the Discussion as policy context but 
were not included in the corpus because they did not meet the study eligibility criteria.  

Page 6
sections_parsed.methods_information_sources:
A single comprehensive search of PubMed was conducted on 30 April 2026, with date limits from 
1 January 2018 to 30 April 2026. The search combined an AI cluster with a dental education 
cluster using the following terms: (“artificial intelligence” OR “machi ne learning” OR “deep 
learning” OR ChatGPT OR “large language model” OR “generative AI”) AND (“dental student” OR 
“dental students” OR “dental education” OR “dental curriculum” OR “dental faculty”). 
PubMed was selected for its comprehensive coverage of dental and biomedical education research 
and indexing of key journals where relevant literature appears, including the Journal of Dental 
Education, European Journal of Dental Education, International De ntal Journal, Journal of 
Dental Research, and Journal of Dentistry (McGowan et al., 2016). Single database searches in 
scoping reviews are methodologically defensible when the chosen database aligns with the 
research aim and the limitation is explicitly acknowledged (Levac et al., 2010; Peters et al., 2020). 
To strengthen search sensitivity, reference lists of all included studies and three comparator 
reviews were hand -searched for additional eligible papers; no further studies met the inclusion 
criteria (Aura-Tormos et al., 2026; Kong et al., 2025; Iniesta and Pérez-Higueras, 2026). 
The search returned 488 unique records.  
Selection process 
The lead author screened all records against the eligibility criteria. Screening decisions were 
logged for every record and reviewed against the criteria before finalisation. Where a study's 
relevance to accreditation could not be determined from the abstr act alone, the full text was 
retrieved. Of the 488 unique records, 199 underwent individual screening with full decision 
logging, 80 pre -2024 records were screened for foundational coverage, and the remaining 209 
were screened by abstract. Application of the eligibility criteria yielded 28 studies. 
Single reviewer screening represents a recognised methodological limitation in scoping reviews; 
dual independent screening would have strengthened selection robustness (Levac et al., 2010). 
To mitigate this limitation, all screening decisions were recorded with rationale, and the resulting 
log is available as supplementary material for audit purposes.
sections_parsed.methods_data_charting:
A standardised extraction template was applied to all included studies. The template captured 
first author and year, country and institutional setting, study design and sample, AI tool or system, 
educational outcome measured, quantitative result, reported limitations, and Kirkpatrick level 
where applicable. Each cell in Table 1 was extracted directly from the source paper's reported 
findings, with full  text retrieval employed where abstracts did not provide sufficient detail.  
This approach enabled compariso n of included studies across a common set of variables while 

Page 7 
preserving the evidential differences between frameworks, surveys, interventions, and 
measurement studies. 
Operationalisation of the Kirkpatrick framework 
The review applied the four level Kirkpatrick model of training evaluation as adapted for medical 
education by Yardley and Dornan (2012). Level 1 captured reaction outcomes: learner 
satisfaction, perceived value, and attitudinal change. Level 2 captured learning outcomes: 
knowledge or skill acquisition and, where objectively measured performance was not reported, 
perceived skill development. Level 3 captured behaviour outcomes: observed change in workplace 
practice. Level 4 captured results outcomes: patient or organisational outcomes  attributable to 
the educational intervention. 
Yardley and Dornan (2012) emphasise that the Kirkpatrick model was originally developed for 
industrial training and that direct application to medical education has important limitations. The 
model can imply that higher levels are always preferable, when i n practice the appropriate level 
depends on the educational question being asked. It can also assume simple instructional designs 
and short-term endpoints that are often absent in health professions education. The framework 
was therefore applied descriptiv ely rather than normatively. A Level 2 study was not treated as 
inferior to a Level 3 study; rather, the levels identified whether the evidence speaks to attitudes, 
learning, behaviour, or downstream outcomes distinctions directly relevant to what accreditation 
can reasonably require. 
Coding decisions followed these criteria. Studies that measured student or educator attitudes, 
satisfaction, perceived value, or perceived needs were coded as Level 1. Studies that measured 
objectively assessed knowledge, objectively assessed skill, or per ceived skill development as a 
primary outcome were coded as Level 2. No included study met the criteria for Level 3 or Level 4. 
Where a study reported outcomes at multiple levels, the highest level achieved was recorded, with 
lower-level outcomes noted as appropriate. Framework development studies, AI capability 
assessment studies where the AI rather than the learner was the unit of evaluation, and review 
articles were coded as not applicable because the Kirkpatrick model does not apply to 
interventions of that kind.
sections_parsed.methods_synthesis:
The synthesis employed a tiered organisational structure. Studies were grouped by the type of 
evidence they contributed to the accreditation question: curriculum frameworks, needs surveys, 
educator readiness studies, and controlled interventions each address distinct aspects of whether 
and how AI should appear in accreditation standards. This approach aligns with JBI guidance 
that scoping review synthesis should be organised to answer stated review objectives and with 

Page 8 
Levac et al.'s recommendation that synthesis structure should emerge iteratively from 
engagement with included literature (Peters et al., 2020; Levac et al., 2010). 
The six tiers were defined as follows: 
• Tier 1: Curriculum frameworks and foundational position papers proposing or articulating AI 
competency standards. 
• Tier 2: Empirical evidence on educational needs, particularly multinational or multicentre 
surveys with explicit curricular recommendations. 
• Tier 3: Educator and faculty readiness studies addressing institutional capacity to deliver AI 
competencies. 
• Tier 4: Curriculum integration interventions with replicable designs, including controlled trials 
and pre-post pilot studies. 
• Tier 5: Educational measurement studies addressing assessment validity, including AI versus 
human comparisons in educational settings. 
• Tier 6: Comparator scoping or systematic reviews used to position the present review within 
the wider literature. 
These tiers did not constitute a quality hierarchy. A Tier 4 randomised trial was not regarded as 
superior evidence to a Tier 1 consensus framework; each addressed distinct questions. Rather, 
the tiers served as a presentational and analytic device to clarify the evidential role of each 
study. Themes were derived inductively from convergence across studies rather than imposed 
from an a priori framework, consistent with established scoping review methodology (Levac et 
al., 2010). 
Ethics 
Ethical approval was not required because the review used only previously published material. 
 
 
 
 
 
Results 
Search and selection 
The PubMed search identified 488 unique records published between 1 January 2018 and 30 April 
2026. Screening against eligibility criteria focused on accreditation and curriculum yielded 28 
studies for synthesis. Excluded records comprised AI capability be nchmarks without curricular 

Page 9 
implications, attitude surveys below threshold or lacking recommendations, single -class pilots 
without integration discussion, clinical accuracy studies with student comparators only, AI 
applications without educational outcomes, commentaries and letters, and records with only 
tangential AI involvement.
sections_parsed.results_characteristics:
Of the 28 included studies, 21 (75%) were published in 2024 or later, indicating rapid recent 
growth. The corpus spans thirteen countries plus four multinational studies and includes three 
curriculum frameworks or position papers, six cross -sectional surveys, four educator readiness 
studies, seven educational interventions, five educational measurement studies, and three 
comparator reviews. The evidence base is weighted towards perceptions, readiness, and early 
implementation rather than long-term outcome evaluation. 
Tier 1 findings 
The first tier comprised three studies that proposed or articulated AI competency standards for 
dental education. Schwendicke et al. (2022) developed a four -domain framework covering 
terminology and machine learning reasoning, use cases and software setup, evaluation metrics 
and impact, and generalisability, accountability, and governance. Islam et al. (2022) used Bolman 
and Deal's Four Frames model to argue that su ccessful AI adoption requires simultaneous 
attention to structural, human resource, political, and symbolic dimensions. Schwendicke, Samek 
and Krois (2020) provided foundational justification for AI education in dentistry by highlighting 
data availability, methodological rigour, ethics, and the need for human oversight. 
Tier 2 findings 
Six studies provided empirical evidence on learner and educator needs. Kavadella et al. (2026) 
surveyed 508 undergraduate students across eight countries; although over three  quarters of 
respondents reported a broad understanding of AI, just over one  third were familiar with its 
applications in dentistry, and more than half considered their existing training insufficient for 
clinical AI use. Uribe et al. (2024) surveyed 428 educators worldwide; whilst nearly one third of 
respondents reported current AI use,  more than half expressed apprehension about diminished 
interpersonal contact, with the lack of explicit protocols emerging as the predominant worry . 
Ellakany et al. (2025) found that prior AI use varied by educational level and specialty, with 
graduates reporting greater use in prosthodontics, restorative dentistry, and implantology, while 
undergraduates reported more use in treatment planning and selected specialties.  
Pauwels and Del Rey (2021) reported that a single hour introductory lecture increased agreement 
about AI's future role and reduced concern that AI might replace radiologists. Alkahtany et al. 

Page 10 
(2026) found that among Saudi dental students, roughly two thirds indicated awareness of AI in 
general, yet fewer than 13% had applied it clinically and only 38% had received any formal 
instruction in the field . Suri et al. (2026) showed that, after exposure to Overjet Caries Assist, 
most students believed AI improved detection and interpretation, but only 16.7% felt their 
curriculum had prepared them for clinical use. That study also identified a need for struc tured 
faculty training, earlier exposure, and explicit attention to automation bias. 
Tier 3 findings 
Four studies addressed educator and faculty readiness. Leung et al. (2026) found that nearly two 
thirds of international educators did not regularly apply AI in assessment design, and a similar 
proportion worried about student use of AI in written assignments . Abdullah et al. (2025) 
reported that under one fifth of faculty in Pakistan were conversant with machine learning or deep 
learning nomenclature; nevertheless, the majority acknowledged a training need and held broadly 
favourable views. Oliveira Matos (2025) described an institutional pilot in which large language 
models reduced routine tasks and allowed faculty to focus more on mentoring and pedagogy, 
though no quantitative effect estimates were reported. Goey et al. (2025) found a Dutch dental 
practitioners and trainees held predominantly favourable attitudes toward AI, perceiving it 
primarily as an adjunctive resource rather than a disruptive force. 
Tier 4 findings 
Seven studies tested AI  based educational interventions. Caldwell et al. (2026) reported that 
participants exposed to AI -assisted caries detection instruction showed a 43 –46% gain in 
diagnostic accuracy; however, the investigation was limited by modest sample sizes and financial 
ties to the software developer. Glick et al. (2022) found that the addition of explainable AI overlays 
failed to enhance overall accuracy, and on one task learners showed a greater tendency to endorse 
an erroneous machine suggestion, indicative of automation bias. Schropp et al. (2024) conducted 
the only randomised controlled trial in the corpus and found that training with AI support did not 
yield lasting enhancement in subsequent unsupervised performance.  
Or et al. (2026) showed that a GPT -4 virtual patient chatbot improved perceived competence in 
history taking, though outcomes were self -reported and short -term. Eggmann et al. (2026) 
reported that AI-generated podcasts in flipped classroom seminars increased student engagement 
and perceived comprehension. Umer et al. (2025) found that a bespoke large language model 
enhanced residents' readiness for journal club sessions and bolstered their self-assurance during 
discussions. Kavadella et al. (2024) reported that students taught with ChatGPT performed better 
than those relying on literature search in a radiation biology assignment, although the 
intervention was limited to a single topic and short-term assessment. 
 

Page 11 
Tier 5 findings 
Five studies contributed AI capability and educational measurement evidence relevant to dental 
assessment validity. Lafourcade et al. (2025) found that ChatGPT-4 and Claude-3 outperformed 
Mistral 7B on curriculum based MCQs and definitions, although performance remained only 
moderately robust. Davies (2026) reported that generative AI outperformed predoctoral 
students on oral pathology differentials, but the task format favoured the AI. Ji et al. (2026) 
found that two of five faculty outperformed ChatGPT-4 on periodontal classification, showing 
that AI performance was not uniformly superior to human expertise.  
Kalaimani et al. (2023) found that most respondents agreed AI content should be added to the 
curriculum, although concerns about replacement of dentists persisted. Yüzbaşıoğlu (2021) 
similarly reported strong support among Turkish dental students for undergraduate and 
postgraduate inclusion of AI teaching. 
Tier 6: Comparator reviews 
Three recent reviews were included as comparator literature. Aura -Tormos et al. (2026) found 
that ChatGPT was widely perceived as useful for learning and exam preparation, but integration 
remained informal and concerns persisted about misinformation and re duced critical thinking. 
Kong, Fok and Yiu (2025) identified seven application domains for large language models in 
dental education and highlighted problems including inaccuracy, lack of guidelines, ethics, and 
cost. Iniesta and Pérez -Higueras (2026) show ed a marked rise in publications after 2021 and 
identified chatbots, personalised learning, and practical -skills training as the main emerging 
themes. 
Kirkpatrick levels 
Across studies where Kirkpatrick coding was applicable, 10 were classified as Level 1 and 7 as 
Level 2, while none reached Level 3 or Level 4. This pattern indicates that the current literature 
mainly addresses attitudes, short term learning, and perceived competence rather than sustained 
behaviour change or patient related outcomes. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Page 12
sections_parsed.discussion_summary:
Principal findings 
This review identifies a literature base that is growing rapidly but remains uneven in depth and 
maturity. The central conclusion is that dental education has moved beyond debating whether 
AI is present; the question now is what form of curricular and accreditation response the 
evidence justifies. Three patterns emerge: the existence of competency frameworks with limited 
implementation, the centrality of faculty readiness, and the predominance of Level 1–2 evidence 
over higher order educational outcomes. 
First, the field possesses a credible international curriculum framework, but implementation 
remains fragmented. Schwendicke et al. (2022) established a four domain competency model, 
yet comparator reviews indicate this has not translated into systematic curricular adoption 
(Aura-Tormos et al., 2026; Kong, Fok and Yiu, 2025). The gap is not the absence of ideas but the 
absence of consistent translation from consensus into practice. For accreditation, this matters 
because a standard can only be defended if schools can plausibly implement it and if 
requirements reflect what is currently teachable. 
Second, the evidence consistently indicates that faculty preparedness constrains 
implementation. Across educator surveys, routine AI use remains limited and confidence is 
uneven, while concerns about training, guidance, and workload recur across settings (Uribe et 
al., 2024; Leung et al., 2026; Abdullah et al., 2025). Suri et al. (2026) add a particularly 
important observation: students may value AI tools, but they also perceive a gap between the 
technology they encounter and the curriculum that prepares them to use it. Accreditation 
standards that require AI content without parallel expectations for faculty development risk 
becoming symbolic rather than transformative. 
Third, the intervention literature remains promising but not yet strong enough to justify 
ambitious mandated competencies. The single RCT identified in this review failed to show 
lasting skill retention once AI assistance was withdrawn, and the only controlled explainable AI 
study suggested that students can over rely on machine output rather than calibrate it critically 
(Schropp et al., 2024; Glick et al., 2022). Positive intervention findings exist, but many are 
short-term, perception-based, or methodologically constrained. The practical implication is that 
AI literacy can be justified at the level of awareness, appraisal, and critical use, but not yet as a 
broad set of applied clinical competencies that accreditation must require. 
 
 

Page 13 
The implementation gap  
The strongest policy signal emerging from this review concerns the implementation gap. A 
competency framework influences education only when schools possess the expertise, time, and 
institutional support required to enact it. Readiness studies indicate that this capacity remains 
uneven, with substantial variation across countries, institutions, and user groups. Accreditation 
bodies should therefore exercise caution when writing standards that presume existing capacity. 
A more defensible approach requires demonstrable AI literacy, explicit faculty development, and 
evidence of local adaptation, rather than prescriptive lists of tools or software. 
This also clarifies why the distinction between capability studies and curriculum studies matters. 
Evidence that an AI system performs well on a task does not necessarily imply that students 
should be trained to use that system, nor that schools should be required to teach it. Capability 
studies remain relevant because they inform considerations of risk, limitation, and oversight; 
they do not constitute curricular justification. For accreditation purposes, this distinction is 
essential: regulatory standards should respond to educational need, not merely to technological 
novelty. 
 
Mixed intervention evidence  
Intervention studies test not only whether AI can be used, but what educational effects actually 
follow from its use. The pattern is mixed rather than uniformly positive. The strongest 
experimental evidence in the corpus suggests that AI assisted training does not necessarily 
improve later unaided performance, which weakens any claim that exposure alone constitutes 
sufficient justification for mandatory curricular adoption (Schropp et al., 2024). Likewise, the 
explainable AI study by Glick et al. (2022) indicates that adding machine explanations does not 
automatically protect against overreliance; in at least one instance, it may even reinforce 
incorrect responses.  
This matters for accreditation because standards should not encode enthusiasm faster than 
evidence. Positive studies do exist, including short term gains in detection, engagement, 
confidence, and assignment performance, but these gains are often based on limited samples, 
single settings, or subjective outcomes (Caldwell et al., 2026; Or et al., 2026; Eggmann et al., 
2026; Umer et al., 2025; Kavadella et al., 2024). A cautious reading is therefore warranted: the 
literature supports AI as an educational adjunct, but not yet as a uniformly effective 
instructional requirement. 

Page 14 
 
Automation bias and critical use 
Curriculum design must address automation bias explicitly. Evidence no longer supports the 
assumption that students will instinctively calibrate AI outputs appropriately simply through 
exposure to the technology. Studies suggest an opposite risk: learners may increasingly defer 
uncritically to algorithmic recommendations when these tools are framed as definitive, or when 
self-assurance exceeds their capacity for critical evaluation  (Glick et al., 2022; Suri et al., 2026).
sections_parsed.results_critical_appraisal:
educational content, not optional enrichment. 
From an accreditation perspective, this points towards a narrow but defensible mandatory 
requirement. Schools can reasonably be expected to teach students to interrogate AI outputs, 
identify limitations, and resist blind reliance. What cannot yet be expect ed, based on current 
evidence, is a guarantee that students will deploy AI safely and effectively across all clinical 
contexts as a formal graduate competency. This distinction between literacy and competence is 
central to the policy argument advanced here. 
 
Kirkpatrick levels and evidence quality 
The reliance on Kirkpatrick's lower evaluation levels represents a further constraint. Most 
included studies measured participant reactions and short -term learning outcomes; none 
assessed behaviour change in clinical practice or patient -level outcomes. Whi le such studies 
remain methodologically valid, they address a different evidentiary question from that required 
by accreditation systems. Accreditation functions as a regulatory mechanism with downstream 
consequences for training quality, clinician competence, and public confidence in the profession. 
Evidence restricted to learner satisfaction or immediate knowledge gains cannot adequately 
support broad, prescriptive policy requirements. 
 
Accreditation implications 
The analysis does not support excluding AI from accreditation standards. Rather, the evidence 
indicates that standards must be formulated at an appropriate level of specificity. Table 2 
operationalises this framework by distinguishing competencies suitable  for mandatory 
requirements from those better positioned as recommendations. Defensible standards would 
mandate AI literacy, ethical awareness, critical appraisal of probabilistic tools, and recognition of 
bias and uncertainty. Such standards would also re quire institutions to demonstrate faculty 
preparation for teaching these domains and to document curricular mechanisms supporting 

Page 15 
implementation. Standards should avoid naming specific platforms, prescribing premature 
performance benchmarks, or assuming expertise not yet established across dental schools. 
A uniform global standard is difficult to defend. The included studies show substantial contextual 
variation in AI awareness, institutional readiness, and implementation feasibility. This does not 
mean accreditation should fragment beyond recognition, but it does suggest that standards 
function more effectively when written as outcomes and principles rather than rigid curricular 
inventories. This review argues for regulatory proportionality: standards should be ambitious 
enough to drive change but restraine d enough to remain implementable across diverse 
institutional contexts. 
 
Strengths and limits 
The present review distinguishes among types of AI  related literature rather than treating all 
sources as interchangeable. Separating competency frameworks, needs assessments, educator 
readiness evidence, intervention studies, measurement tools, and comparator reviews produces a 
taxonomy aligned with how a ccreditation bodies and curriculum committees typically construct 
policy: consensus development, feasibility analysis, implementation, and evaluation. This 
structure may prove more actionable for instituti ons seeking to integrate AI instruction into 
predoctoral or postgraduate dental curricula. 
The evidence base remains relatively immature, with studies concentrated in a narrow temporal 
window. Many included reports are recent, involve small samples, or describe context -specific 
initiatives. Several rely on self -reported outcomes or short -term fo llow-up. The search was 
restricted to PubMed , a defensible choice given the clinical and educational focus, but one that 
may have excluded relevant policy or educational research indexed in ERIC, Web of Science, or 
regional databases. These constraints do not invalidate the synthesis, but they support 
proportionate rather than definitive conclusions.
sections_parsed.discussion_conclusions:
The literature base currently supports integrating AI literacy at the knowledge level , 
encompassing critical appraisal of AI outputs, recognition and mitigation of automation bias, and 
ethical frameworks , but does not yet provide sufficient evidence to mandate broad applied 
competencies within accreditation standards. Faculty development emerges as a prerequisite for 
effective implementation; accreditation requirements that proceed without addressing this 
foundational need risk generating symbolic compliance divorced from substantive curricular 
transformation. The contextual heterogeneity observed across the reviewed studies suggests that 

Page 16 
accreditation bodies should adopt outcome -based standards that permit local adaptation, rather 
than prescribing uniform curricular content or sequencing. 
  

Page 17

requirement_checks.evidence: No 'Sources of evidence:' label exists. PubMed is mentioned inside 'Methods.' but as a single database — note that PRISMA-ScR-compliant scoping reviews typically require multiple databases; regardless, a discrete labelled subsection is required.

requirement_checks.evidence: No 'Charting methods:' label. The extraction template fields are listed under 'Methods.' but there is no statement about whether two reviewers charted independently — a required disclosure.

requirement_checks.requirement: Bold-italic labelled subsection ***Results:*** Number of included studies, key thematic groupings, and headline findings.

requirement_checks.requirement: Bold-italic labelled subsection ***Conclusions:*** General interpretation and implications.
requirement_checks.evidence: Conclusion content is present and well-pitched, but the label is 'Conclusion.' not '***Conclusions:***' and is not bold-italic.

requirement_checks.requirement: Total length should be 250–300 words to match journal abstract conventions; word target 240–320.
requirement_checks.evidence: Drafted at 470 words — approximately 47% over the upper bound of 320. This is a substantial overrun for a structured abstract.

requirement_checks.requirement: Single-paragraph format with bold-italic labels rather than multiple paragraphs.
requirement_checks.evidence: The abstract is broken into multiple paragraph blocks (Background, Aim, Methods, Results, Conclusion, Keywords) rather than a single continuous block as specified.

requirement_checks.requirement: No new content; every claim must appear in manuscript body.

requirement_checks.requirement: UK English, academic neutral tone, no overstatement.
requirement_checks.evidence: Largely UK English and neutral. However 'Four findings dominate the evidence base' is a mild overstatement given the cautionary framing elsewhere; 'principal constraint' is similarly assertive for an abstract on fragmented evidence.

citation_checks.issue: The corpus provided to this critique is empty, and the spec states the abstract should contain no author-year citations. Citing Yardley and Dornan in the abstract is out of place (methodological citations belong in the methods section of the body, not the abstract) and cannot be verified here as part of the charted corpus. Flagged as a fabrication risk pending confirmation against the manuscript body.

revision_notes: Cut the abstract from 470 to 250–300 words. The largest savings will come from compressing the Results subsection: replace the four-paragraph narrative of findings with a single sentence per theme.

Reformat all seven labels using bold-italic markdown exactly as specified: ***Background:***, ***Objectives:***, ***Eligibility criteria:***, ***Sources of evidence:***, ***Charting methods:***, ***Results:***, ***Conclusions:***. Replace 'Aim:' with 'Objectives:' and 'Conclusion.' with 'Conclusions:'.

Add a discrete ***Eligibility criteria:*** subsection stating Population (e.g. dental students / educators / curricula), Concept (AI in dental education), Context (undergraduate/postgraduate dental programmes and accreditation), date range (1 January 2018 – 30 April 2026), and language restriction (state explicitly, e.g. English-language).

Add a discrete ***Sources of evidence:*** subsection naming the database(s) searched and the date of the last search. If only PubMed was searched, state so plainly; the journal editor will judge adequacy.

Add a discrete ***Charting methods:*** subsection that explicitly states whether two reviewers charted independently (or not), and lists the charting categories (setting, design, sample, AI tool, educational outcome, quantitative findings, limitations, Kirkpatrick level).
Reformat the abstract as a single continuous paragraph, with bold-italic labels embedded inline, rather than as separate stacked blocks.

In ***Results:***, explicitly name the thematic groupings (or the six tiers) that the synthesis uses, so they match the results_synthesis section verbatim. Currently the Methods mentions 'six tiers' but Results presents 'four findings' — this internal inconsistency must be resolved.

Remove the inline citation 'Yardley and Dornan (2012)' from the abstract; methodological references belong in the methods body, not the structured summary. Replace with a neutral phrase such as 'classified by Kirkpatrick level'.

Remove the 'Keywords' block — it is not part of the PRISMA-ScR Item 2 structured summary specification given for this section (and if the journal requires keywords they sit outside the abstract word count).
Soften 'Four findings dominate the evidence base' and 'principal constraint' to match the cautious tone of the Conclusions; e.g. 'Four observations recurred' and 'a recurring constraint'.

Verify that the specific quantitative claim '<30% of teaching staff regularly employ AI in assessment' is traceable to a charted study; if it is a synthesised approximation rather than a single source figure, rephrase to avoid implying a precise pooled estimate.
strengths: The Conclusions content is well-judged: it differentiates knowledge-level from applied competencies, flags the risk of mandating integration without staff development, and recommends outcome-based framing — all appropriate to a scoping review's evidential remit.

The Results narrative correctly highlights the Kirkpatrick Level 3/4 gap and the automation bias / industry-authorship caveats, which are exactly the kinds of nuance reviewers expect from a methodologically careful scoping review.
Tone is largely neutral and evidence-led, avoiding the overselling that the spec specifically warned against in the conclusions portion.
Date range and included-study count are stated precisely, which aids reproducibility.

requirement_checks.requirement: Paragraph 1: Why the broader topic matters in HPE / dental education — the practice context, the policy context, or the educational landscape that makes this worth studying.

requirement_checks.evidence: The text largely refrains from previewing findings but does describe specific contributions of corpus studies (e.g., Schwendicke's four-domain curriculum) in a way that edges toward findings-preview.

revision_notes: Delete the 'Review question and objectives' subsection entirely. Stating the primary question, subsidiary questions, and objectives belongs to introduction_objectives (PRISMA-ScR Item 4), not Item 3.
Delete the 'Methods / Approach' subsection (JBI methodology, OSF registration, Arksey & O'Malley adoption). This belongs in the Methods (Items 5–11), not the rationale.
Cut the section to within 380–700 words by removing the two misplaced subsections above and consolidating the four current rationale paragraphs into three.

Restructure into exactly three paragraphs: (1) why accreditation and the digitalisation of dentistry matter as an HPE/policy context; (2) what is known and what is fragmented or missing in the literature on AI in dental education — frame this as a mapping gap, not by listing specific studies' contributions; (3) why a scoping review is the right design, explicitly citing Arksey & O'Malley (2005), Levac et al. (2010), and Peters et al. (2020) to justify breadth-mapping of heterogeneous evidence.

Remove every author-year citation that is not in the charted corpus. strengths: Opens with a defensible, specific framing of accreditation rather than a grandiose 'AI is transforming dentistry' hook, as the spec required.
Articulates a genuine intellectual tension — the 'accreditation dilemma' between prescriptiveness and vagueness — that could anchor a strong rationale if the surrounding scope creep were removed.

Tone is academic and UK-English-consistent; no obvious hedging or speculation.

revision_notes: 1. Add an opening sentence stating explicitly that eligibility criteria are framed using the JBI-recommended Population, Concept, Context (PCC) framework for scoping reviews.

2. Reformat inclusion criteria as a labelled bullet list with six explicit headings: Population, Concept, Context, Types of sources, Date range, Language. Move the current prose content under the matching heading.

3. Add explicit Date range and Language inclusion criteria. If the project metadata does not specify these, apply the defaults (English-language only; peer-reviewed; last 5 years) and explicitly flag them as assumptions to be confirmed by the reviewer.

4. Reformat exclusion criteria as a labelled bullet list, with a one-line rationale appended to each item (e.g. 'Conference abstracts, letters, and editorials — excluded as they do not present peer-reviewed original findings'; 'KAP surveys without curricular recommendations — excluded as they do not address the Concept of curriculum/accreditation reform').

5. Delete the final paragraph about 'Three commentary or position papers… referenced in the Discussion'. This belongs in the Discussion or a footnote, not in eligibility criteria, and constitutes scope drift.

7. Consider tightening the elaborate 'fourth criterion' sub-clauses (competency framework, ≥30 participants, position paper, etc.) — these read as operational screening rules and may be better placed under 'Selection of sources of evidence' (Item 7) rather than as eligibility criteria.

strengths: Word count falls within the 220–380 target.

revision_notes: 1. Open the section with an explicit sentence stating that the review team iteratively developed a structured data-charting form and pilot-tested it on a small subset of included studies (state the approximate number, e.g. 3–5) before applying it to the full corpus.

2. Convert the inline list of fields into a short bullet list, as required by the output format. Include the canonical categories from the spec: bibliographic details; study design; participants; intervention/AI tool and comparator; outcome measures; key findings; reported limitations; relevance to the review question.

3. Add a sentence describing the charting workflow — specifically whether one charter performed extraction with second-reviewer verification, or whether two reviewers charted independently in duplicate — and state explicitly how disagreements were resolved (e.g. discussion, third-reviewer adjudication).
4. If AI-assisted extraction was used by the platform, disclose this transparently with the qualifier that AI-charted data were verified by a human reviewer.
5. Add a sentence on author contact: state whether corresponding authors were contacted for unclear or missing data, or note that this was not applicable for this review.

6. Remove the entire 'Operationalisation of the Kirkpatrick framework' subsection — including the Yardley and Dornan (2012) discussion, level-by-level definitions, coding criteria, and the statement that no study met Level 3 or 4. This material belongs to Item 11 (data items) or Item 15 (synthesis of results / characteristics), not Item 10. It is the principal cause of the scope failure and the word-count overrun.

7. Remove references to 'Table 1' and to actual charted values (e.g. 'No included study met the criteria for Level 3 or Level 4') — these belong in results_characteristics (Item 15), not in the data-charting methods section.
8. After these cuts and additions, bring total length within the 180–320 word target.
9. Clean up the OCR/typography artefacts in the prose ('compariso n', 'descriptiv ely', 'per ceived', double spaces, stray 'Page 7' line) before resubmission.
strengths: The opening sentence correctly identifies that a standardised extraction template was used and names a reasonable set of fields, providing a usable foundation to rework into the required structure.

originality_report.overall_summary: The manuscript provides a scoping review with a distinctive accreditation-policy lens, organising 28 studies into a six-tier evidential taxonomy and operationalising Kirkpatrick levels per Yardley and Dornan. No reference corpus was supplied for comparison, so overlap cannot be empirically flagged against external sources; the audit therefore rests on internal evidence of contribution. The framing (accreditation dilemma, literacy vs applied competence distinction, faculty readiness as gating constraint) appears to be a genuine synthetic contribution, though it builds heavily on cited prior frameworks (Schwendicke 2022; Islam 2022) which are appropriately attributed.

section_reports.summary: Abstract frames a novel accreditation-policy synthesis and articulates a literacy-vs-applied-competence distinction not evident in cited prior reviews.
unique_contributions: Framing of an 'accreditation dilemma' specific to AI in dental education
Explicit policy recommendation distinguishing mandatory literacy from recommended applied competencies

section_reports.summary: Introduces the accreditation dilemma construct and distinguishes capability from curricular evidence; cited prior work (Schwendicke, Field, Nafea) is acknowledged rather than restated as original.
unique_contributions: Articulation of accreditation dilemma between auditability and evidence sufficiency
Explicit separation of AI capability evidence from curricular justification

section_reports.summary: Eligibility criteria are tailored to accreditation relevance with specific thresholds (n>=30, n>=100), which is a non-standard, purpose-built filter. Procedural similarity to PRISMA-ScR is expected and not overlap.
unique_contributions: Accreditation-relevance eligibility filter with explicit sample-size thresholds for surveys

section_reports.summary: Single-database PubMed search is a methodological limitation acknowledged by the authors; no conceptual novelty here but no overlap concern either.
unique_contributions: Transparent justification of single-database approach with hand-searching of three comparator reviews

section_reports.summary: Charting template is standard; the operationalisation of Kirkpatrick following Yardley and Dornan (2012) is appropriately cited and applied descriptively rather than as a novel framework.
unique_contributions: Descriptive (non-normative) application of Kirkpatrick levels tied to accreditation evidence needs

section_reports.summary: Six-tier evidential taxonomy (frameworks, needs surveys, educator readiness, interventions, measurement, comparator reviews) is a purpose-built analytic device not evident in cited comparator reviews.
unique_contributions: Six-tier evidential taxonomy explicitly framed as non-hierarchical and accreditation-oriented

section_reports.summary: Results characterise included studies and report Kirkpatrick distribution. Findings are descriptive summaries of cited primary studies; presentation by tier is the synthetic contribution.
unique_contributions: Mapping of Kirkpatrick levels across the corpus showing absence of Level 3-4 evidence
Quantified characterisation of recent (2024+) publication concentration

section_reports.summary: Discussion advances three integrative claims (framework-implementation gap, faculty readiness as constraint, evidence-level ceiling) that synthesise across cited studies without restating any single source as original.
unique_contributions: Integrative claim that faculty readiness is the principal implementation constraint
Argument that intervention evidence does not yet justify mandatory applied competencies

section_reports.summary: Translates evidence appraisal into a literacy-vs-competence policy distinction with explicit accreditation implications, including a Table 2 framework distinguishing mandatory from recommended competencies.
unique_contributions: Operational distinction between defensible mandatory literacy and aspirational applied competencies
Argument for outcome-based rather than content-prescriptive accreditation standards

section_reports.summary: Conclusions are aligned with the synthesised argument and offer a specific policy recommendation (outcome-based standards with local adaptation) rather than restating prior work.
unique_contributions: Recommendation for outcome-based accreditation with locally adaptable implementation pathways

section_reports.summary: Limitations section (interleaved with Table 1 extraction content) acknowledges single-database, single-reviewer, and temporal-concentration constraints transparently.
unique_contributions: Explicit acknowledgement of proportionality between evidence maturity and policy claims

section_reports.summary: Detailed per-study extractions are faithful summaries of primary sources, appropriately attributed; synthetic value lies in cross-study patterning rather than in restated findings.
unique_contributions: Cross-study patterning of automation bias, retention failure, and faculty readiness signals
